summary:
Deng Xiaoping's ascent to paramount leadership of China following Mao Zedong's death in 1976 — consolidated through the late 1970s after defeating the Gang of Four — initiated the most consequential economic transformation of the late twentieth century. Deng rejected Maoist ideological rigidity in favor of pragmatic economic development, encapsulated in his famous maxims about crossing the river by feeling for stones and his indifference to the color of the cat as long as it caught mice. The reform program he launched from 1978 onward introduced market mechanisms into what had been a centrally planned economy.

Agricultural decollectivization under the household responsibility system restored individual farming incentives and dramatically increased food production. [[event:3651|Special Economic Zones]] were established in coastal areas to attract foreign investment under market conditions. State enterprises were given greater autonomy. Over two decades, China achieved average annual GDP growth of nearly ten percent, lifting hundreds of millions of people out of poverty in the largest and fastest reduction of poverty in human history. By 2000, China had been transformed from one of the world's most closed economies into the world's manufacturing workshop and the fourth largest economy globally.

Deng's model combined economic liberalization with firm Communist Party political control, explicitly rejecting the political liberalization that accompanied economic reform in Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union. The [[event:2690|Tiananmen Square crackdown]] of 1989 demonstrated his willingness to use force to maintain political order, while his 1992 southern inspection tour confirmed his commitment to economic opening after a period of retrenchment. The Deng model — market economics under authoritarian political control — became an influential template studied by other developing countries seeking rapid economic growth without political democratization.
